entries.description: Fix ErrorBoundary class by making it extend React.Component. The original class was a plain class, which caused errors because `setState` and `props` are features of React components and were not available on the class instance. This change provides the necessary React component functionality.

Added the `VIEW_SETTINGS` permission to the `receptionist` role. This allows reception staff to access the "Customizations" page to personalize their interface settings, aligning their capabilities with other user roles who have similar access.

Corrected a discrepancy in the backend RBAC mirror by adding the `VIEW_SETTINGS` permission for the `receptionist` role. This aligns the backend authorization logic with the frontend permissions defined in `utils/rbac.ts`, ensuring consistent access control across the application.

entries.description: Replaced the direct usage of `React.useContext(AuthContext)!` with the custom `useAuth()` hook. This improves type safety by ensuring the context is always accessed within its provider and removes the need for the non-null assertion operator (`!`), leading to cleaner and more robust code.

Corrected the route for `/customizations` in the navigation links within `Layout.tsx`. The link previously pointed to `/settings`, which was an incorrect path for the Customizations page, preventing users from navigating to it.

// components/ErrorBoundary.tsx
import React from 'react';
import { AlertTriangle, RefreshCw } from 'lucide-react';

interface ErrorBoundaryProps {
  children?: React.ReactNode;
}

interface ErrorBoundaryState {
  hasError: boolean;
  error: Error | null;
}

/**
 * ErrorBoundary component to catch rendering errors in the component tree.
 */
// Fix: Ensure the class extends React.Component to have state and lifecycle methods.
export class ErrorBoundary extends React.Component<ErrorBoundaryProps, ErrorBoundaryState> {
  // Fix: Initialize state as a class property for modern syntax,
  // removing the need for a constructor.
  state: ErrorBoundaryState = {
    hasError: false,
    error: null,
  };

  /**
   * Static method to update state when an error occurs during rendering.
   */
  static getDerivedStateFromError(error: Error): ErrorBoundaryState {
    return { hasError: true, error };
  }

  /**
   * Lifecycle method to log error details.
   */
  componentDidCatch(error: Error, errorInfo: React.ErrorInfo) {
    console.error('Uncaught error:', error, errorInfo);
  }

  /**
   * Resets the error state to allow the application to attempt re-rendering.
   */
  handleReload = () => {
    // Fix: Calling `setState` requires the class to be a React Component.
    this.setState({ hasError: false, error: null });
  };

  render(): React.ReactNode {
    if (this.state.hasError) {
      return (
        <div className="min-h-screen flex items-center justify-center bg-slate-50 dark:bg-slate-900 p-4">
          <div className="bg-white dark:bg-slate-800 p-8 rounded-2xl shadow-xl max-w-md w-full text-center border border-slate-200 dark:border-slate-700">
            <div className="w-16 h-16 bg-red-100 dark:bg-red-900/20 rounded-full flex items-center justify-center mx-auto mb-4 text-red-600 dark:text-red-400">
              <AlertTriangle size={32} />
            </div>
            <h1 className="text-xl font-bold text-slate-900 dark:text-white mb-2">Something went wrong</h1>
            <p className="text-slate-500 dark:text-slate-400 mb-6 text-sm">
              The application encountered an unexpected error.
            </p>

            {this.state.error && (
              <div className="bg-slate-100 dark:bg-slate-950 p-3 rounded-lg text-left text-xs font-mono text-red-600 dark:text-red-400 overflow-auto max-h-32 mb-6">
                {this.state.error.toString()}
              </div>
            )}

            <button
              onClick={this.handleReload}
              className="inline-flex items-center justify-center gap-2 px-6 py-3 bg-primary-600 hover:bg-primary-700 text-white font-semibold rounded-xl transition-all w-full"
            >
              <RefreshCw size={18} />
              Try Again
            </button>
          </div>
        </div>
      );
    }

    // Fix: Accessing `this.props` requires the class to be a React Component.
    return this.props.children;
  }
}

entries.description: Ensured the ErrorBoundary class correctly extends React.Component, which provides access to state, props, and lifecycle methods like `setState` and `componentDidCatch`. This resolves errors where `props` and `setState` would be undefined on a plain class instance.

Corrected a logical error in the activity stream generation where the 'status' property was missing for patient records. This ensures that every record has a consistent 'status' field, preventing potential runtime errors when rendering status badges in the UI.
Added new translation keys for the Records screen to the Arabic locale file, covering stats, filters, table headers, and modal content for a complete localization experience.

**Investigating Syntax Error**

I'm currently focused on the 'Uncaught SyntaxError' the user reported. It usually stems from a typo, like a misplaced comma, an extra character, or an unclosed bracket. Right now, I'm checking the user's files and I am taking a look at `pages/Records.tsx`.


**Pinpointing the Root Cause**

I've just narrowed down the cause of the syntax error. It's almost certainly in `pages/Records.tsx`. It appears the file got overwritten with the content of `locales/ar.json`, which is just a JSON object. This is invalid code for a `.tsx` file. The original prompt involved updating localization, and it looks like a faulty bot response pasted the content into the wrong file. My next step is to reconstruct the correct `pages/Records.tsx` content.


**Reconstructing the Component**

I'm now reconstructing `pages/Records.tsx`. Examining the application structure, I've confirmed that the 'Records' functionality is integrated as an 'Activity Log' tab within the Reports page. This explains the absence of a dedicated API endpoint for 'Records'. Now, I'm adapting the component structure based on this insight.


**Developing a Unified Log**

I'm now focused on the missing `/records` API endpoint. While the 'Activity Log' exists within the Reports page, the underlying design suggests a comprehensive, system-wide audit trail is needed. I'm investigating how the existing data, currently used in the 'Activity Log', can be expanded and exposed through a new API endpoint. This will provide the necessary data for a dedicated 'Records' page, as outlined in the requirements. This design is similar to the existing 'Activity Log' in the Reports page, but I'm extending it for comprehensive records. I'm focusing on creating a structured unified data format to ensure consistency.

entries.description: Fixes 'setState' and 'props' not existing on type 'ErrorBoundary' by adding an explicit constructor. This ensures the component's `this` context is correctly initialized, which is a more robust pattern than relying on class property initializers that might not be correctly handled by all build configurations.
Fix: Added 'Users' to the lucide-react import to resolve 'Cannot find name Users' error.
